subject: Blocking August sun before it hits the glass
preheader: Why exterior shade beats curtains, plus cheap fixes you can make this month.
title: Sun, windows, and shade: low-cost ways to block heat
introHtml: August sun is doing more work on your cooling bill than the outdoor temperature is. Every square foot of glass facing the afternoon sun acts like a small space heater. This week: how to stop that heat before it gets inside — mostly with things you can do yourself for very little money.
articleHtml:
Stop the sun before it hits the glass

Once sunlight passes through a window, it turns into heat trapped in the room — the greenhouse effect — and curtains can't undo it. Interior shades only intercept heat that's already inside. Exterior shading blocks it before it enters, which is why the same effort spent outside the glass does far more than the same effort inside.

  • Awnings and overhangs work best on south-facing windows. Summer sun rides high, so a modest projection shades the whole pane in July yet still lets in the low winter sun.
  • West windows are the troublemakers. Late-day sun comes in low and nearly horizontal, so overhangs do little there. Use exterior roll shades, shutters, solar screens, or plantings instead.
  • Plant for the long game. A deciduous tree or tall shrubs on the west side give summer shade, then drop their leaves for winter light.
  • Window film is the budget middle ground: it rejects solar heat right at the glass. Match the film to your glass type — some films can stress older insulated units.

If exterior isn't an option

White-backed cellular shades, mounted tight to the frame and closed before the sun arrives — not after the room is hot — are the best interior performers. Timing matters as much as the product.

And if a window feels hot to your hand on a sunny afternoon, that's the one to fix first. More seasonal pointers on our blog, and a rundown of what we do at our services page.
tips: Close shades before the sun reaches the glass, not after the room is already warm.
Touch test: a window that's hot to your hand on a sunny afternoon is your first target.
Solar screens mount like insect screens — take them down in fall to reclaim winter sun.
